title: Sublinear-Memory Confidence Sequences for Streaming Quantiles
description: We propose AdaQuantCS, an algorithm for anytime-valid quantile inference from streaming data using sublinear memory. By maintaining confidence sequences for the CDF at an adaptive grid of thresholds organized in doubling epochs, we invert CDF bounds to obtain valid quantile confidence intervals in O(k log t) memory, compared to O(t) for existing methods.
motivation: Streaming quantile estimation is critical in production monitoring, A/B testing, and risk assessment, but existing methods offer either bounded memory with no valid confidence intervals (quantile sketches like KLL, DDSketch) or valid confidence intervals with unbounded O(n) memory (confidence sequences, Howard & Ramdas 2022). No method bridges this gap. As streams run indefinitely in practice, the O(n) memory requirement of confidence sequences makes them impractical, while sketch-only approaches cannot support calibrated alerting or hypothesis testing.
proposed_approach: AdaQuantCS tracks the CDF F(q) = P(X <= q) at a small adaptive grid of threshold values using Bernoulli confidence sequences, each requiring O(1) memory. The algorithm operates in doubling epochs: at each epoch boundary, it places k thresholds around the current quantile estimate based on previous epochs' CIs. Quantile confidence intervals are obtained by inverting the CDF confidence sequences. Since threshold placement depends only on prior-epoch data, validity is preserved. Intersecting CIs across epochs provides multi-resolution refinement. Total memory is O(k log t) for k thresholds and O(log t) epochs.

related_work.title: Sequential estimation of quantiles with applications to A/B-testing and best-arm identification
related_work.relationship: Provides the foundational confidence sequences for quantiles that our work makes memory-efficient. Their method requires O(t) memory for the empirical CDF; we achieve O(k log t) via adaptive CDF tracking.

related_work.title: Time-uniform, nonparametric, nonasymptotic confidence sequences
related_work.authors: Steven R. Howard, Aaditya Ramdas, Jon McAuliffe, Jasjeet Sekhon
related_work.relationship: Provides the Bernoulli confidence sequences we use as building blocks for CDF tracking at each threshold. Our contribution is the adaptive grid strategy that makes these practical in bounded memory.

related_work.title: Optimal Quantile Approximation in Streams
related_work.authors: Zohar Karnin, Kevin Lang, Edo Liberty
related_work.relationship: Proves optimal O(1/epsilon * log log(1/delta)) space for streaming quantile point estimation (KLL sketch). Our work addresses a different problem: confidence intervals rather than point estimates, with anytime-valid rather than per-query guarantees.

related_work.title: Space-efficient online computation of quantile summaries
related_work.relationship: Foundational work on deterministic streaming quantile estimation (GK sketch). Our work builds on the streaming algorithms tradition but targets confidence intervals with coverage guarantees.

related_work.title: DDSketch: A fast and fully-mergeable quantile sketch with relative-error guarantees
related_work.authors: Charles Masson, Jee E Rim, Homin K. Lee
related_work.relationship: Introduces relative-error quantile sketches widely used in industry. Our method can use DDSketch as an initial estimator for grid placement, and we compare against DDSketch-based baselines.

related_work.title: Time-uniform confidence bands for the CDF under nonstationarity
related_work.relationship: Extends CDF confidence bands to nonstationary settings. Their importance-weighted CS could replace our Bernoulli CS within epochs to handle distribution drift, making our approaches complementary.

related_work.title: Game-theoretic statistics and safe anytime-valid inference
related_work.authors: Aaditya Ramdas, Peter Grunwald, Vladimir Vovk, Glenn Shafer
related_work.relationship: Surveys the SAVI framework (e-values, e-processes, confidence sequences) that provides the theoretical foundation for our anytime-valid guarantees.

hypothesis: An adaptive CDF-tracking algorithm using O(k) thresholds per doubling epoch can produce anytime-valid confidence intervals for streaming quantiles with widths converging at the optimal LIL rate sqrt(t^{-1} log log t), while using O(k log t) total memory -- exponentially less than the O(t) required by existing confidence sequence methods -- without sacrificing coverage validity.
success_criteria: Empirical coverage rate >= (1-alpha-0.01) across all tested distributions (Gaussian, Exponential, Pareto, Student-t) and quantile levels (median, 95th, 99th percentile), verified over 1000 independent trials
CI widths converge at O(sqrt(log log t / t)) rate, matching full-memory baseline within a constant factor (at most 2x wider for k >= 50)
Memory usage confirmed to be O(k log t), achieving >= 100x reduction vs. full-memory methods at stream length t = 10^6
Adaptive grid outperforms fixed grid by >= 30% in CI width for the same memory budget
Total experimental runtime under 8 hours on 2 CPU cores
